food_subgroup: One-pot claypot rice dish with chicken, Chinese sausage (lap cheong), and salted fish; cooked in an unglazed clay pot over open flame

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for Cantonese claypot rice dishes. Closest parent would be a cooked rice dish concept. No defensible exact URI available; null used to preserve ontology honesty.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based mixed dish — closest FoodEx2 parent for a cooked rice dish with meat and fish toppings. No specific claypot rice code exists in FoodEx2; A000E (composite rice dish) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Cantonese claypot rice. A000E used as the closest defensible composite rice dish anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for claypot rice as a specific food item.

preparation_method: cooked in clay pot over open flame

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one individual claypot of approximately 450g, as typically served at Singapore hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Claypot Rice — Chicken and Salted Fish — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) for claypot rice with chicken and salted fish. Values represent the composite dish as served at Singapore hawker stalls.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white rice and analogous cooked rice dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 75 assigned based on white rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database (white rice GI range 64–80, mean ~72–75). The caramelised scorched rice bottom (guō bā) is expected to have a marginally higher GI than plain cooked white rice due to the Maillard reaction and dextrinisation of starch during high-heat caramelisation. GI 75 is a defensible upper-range estimate for this preparation. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (75 × 90) / 100 = 67.5. Carbohydrate per serving = 20.0 g/100g × 450g = 90g.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium: 1,215mg per claypot (270mg/100g) — 61% of the 2,000mg daily RDI from a single meal, driven by salted fish (~450–750mg), Chinese sausage (~300mg), and soy-based sauces (~200–300mg)
hypertension.guidance: Claypot rice with salted fish is contraindicated for patients with severe hypertension; for mild-to-moderate hypertension, consumption should be occasional and limited to half a portion (225g, ~608mg sodium) with explicit counselling on cumulative daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load of 67.5 per claypot (GI ~75, 90g available carbohydrate per serving) — among the highest GL single-meal values in Singapore hawker cuisine, equivalent to consuming more than two bowls of plain cooked white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Claypot rice should be strictly limited for patients with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed, portion must be reduced to no more than half a claypot (225g, GL ~34), the high-GI crispy scorched rice bottom (guō bā) should be avoided, and the meal should be paired with non-starchy vegetables and protein to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined cardiovascular risk from high sodium (1,215mg/serving), saturated fat from Chinese sausage (lap cheong contains ~30g fat/100g, predominantly saturated), cholesterol (99mg/serving), and the IARC Group 1 carcinogen status of Cantonese-style salted fish (associated with nasopharyngeal carcinoma at regular consumption)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of claypot rice with salted fish due to the compounding risks of high sodium, saturated fat from lap cheong, and the carcinogenic risk of Cantonese-style salted fish; occasional consumption at reduced portions with modified ingredients (reduced salted fish, leaner protein) may be considered under dietitian guidance.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cantonese-style salted fish is an IARC Group 1 carcinogen; high saturated fat from lap cheong; high sodium load — all relevant to systemic inflammation and RA management
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should strictly limit claypot rice with salted fish due to the pro-inflammatory saturated fat content, high sodium, and the carcinogenic risk of regular salted fish consumption; less processed alternatives are strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Claypot rice with chicken and salted fish (瓦煲饭) is one of Singapore's most iconic Cantonese comfort foods. Raw white rice, marinated chicken pieces, sliced Chinese sausage (lap cheong), and small portions of Cantonese-style salted fish are placed together in an unglazed clay pot and cooked over charcoal or gas flame. The uneven heat distribution of the clay pot creates a prized crispy caramelised rice crust at the bottom known as guō bā (锅巴), which is considered the most flavourful part of the dish. The dish is finished with oyster sauce, soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and sesame oil drizzled over the top before serving. Nutritionally, claypot rice with salted fish presents three major clinical concerns: (1) Extremely high sodium at 1,215mg per claypot (270mg/100g), representing 61% of the 2,000mg daily RDI from a single meal — driven by the salted fish, lap cheong, and soy-based sauces; (2) Very high glycemic load of 67.5 per claypot (GI ~75, 90g carbohydrate per 450g serving), making it one of the highest GL single-meal dishes in Singapore hawker cuisine; (3) Cantonese-style salted fish is classified by IARC as a Group 1 carcinogen associated with nasopharyngeal carcinoma at regular consumption. For patients with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension, this dish represents the worst combination of high GL and high sodium in a single hawker meal. The crispy rice bottom (guō bā), while culturally prized, contributes the highest-GI component. Per 100g: 150 kcal, 6g protein, 5g fat (1.9g saturated), 20g carbohydrate, 270mg sodium.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) — the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. GI assigned as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database white rice values (GI 75, upper defensible range for caramelised rice preparation). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are present and internally consistent. Serving size of 450g is consistent with standard Singapore hawker claypot rice portions.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite claypot rice dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) as of April 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per HPB source for this dish. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; the caramelised scorched rice bottom (guō bā) is expected to have a marginally higher GI than plain cooked white rice, making 75 a defensible upper-range estimate. GL per serving = (75 × 90) / 100 = 67.5, where 90g carbohydrate = 20g/100g × 450g serving. Cantonese-style salted fish is classified by IARC as a Group 1 carcinogen (Monograph Vol. 56, 1993; reaffirmed in subsequent reviews) specifically associated with nasopharyngeal carcinoma at regular consumption — this is documented in health_context for cardiovascular_disease, rheumatoid_arthritis, and hypertension conditions. FoodEx2 code A000E used as closest defensible parent for composite rice dish; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for claypot rice. food_group corrected from input 'Chinese Soups & Porridge' to 'Grains and staples' as claypot rice is a rice-based one-pot dish, not a soup or porridge.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from 'Chinese Soups & Porridge' to 'Grains and staples' to accurately reflect dish category.
Added Chinese traditional local name (瓦煲飯（雞肉鹹魚）) in addition to simplified.
Added full Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names with dish-specific descriptors.
Expanded aliases_by_language to include all mandatory Singaporean dish languages with multiple entries per language.
Verified all aliases_by_language string values appear verbatim in flat aliases array (q8 consistency check).
Documented -1 fields (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g) in notes with source and reason.
Documented trans_fat_g 0.0 as confirmed negligible per HPB source.
Added Cardiovascular Disease to health_context (was missing from input — only diabetes, hypertension, rheumatoid_arthritis were present).
Renamed health_context key from 'diabetes' to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per scoring model requirement.
Added gi_source as structured object with University of Sydney GI Database reference and full analogous estimate documentation.
Added FoodEx2 A000E as closest defensible ontology anchor with gap note.
Expanded llm_training.natural_language_description to exceed 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
Added ingredient_synonyms for guō bā, lap cheong, and salted fish in llm_training.
Corrected food_name to canonical form without em-dash and parenthetical serving size.
